hook: Voss asks Jamie directly whether he has found anything unusual in the house — a question that sounds polite but isn't.
goal: Jamie must get Voss out of the house without revealing he has the box, then open it alone and find out what it contains.

items: old video camera (wrapped in dark cloth, in bag)
small metal box (wrapped in grey cloth, in bag, lock still rusted shut)
flat-head screwdriver and other clearing tools in tool bag
knowledge: The Warners — Frank, his wife, and daughter — vanished overnight from this house in 1974
The camera footage shows Frank Warner hiding something beneath the third floorboard
The floorboard had been disturbed before Jamie arrived — someone found the hiding spot recently
The metal box has a rusted lock he has not yet opened
Voss's eyes went directly to the exact floorboard spot at the end of Episode 2
suspicions: Voss is the person who disturbed the floorboard before Jamie arrived
Voss came to the house today specifically to look for the box
Mrs. Holt knows more about the Warners' disappearance than she has said

continuity_rules: Must begin immediately after Voss's eyes moved to the floorboard spot — do not re-show that discovery
Must not repeat the information that the Warners vanished in 1974 as a new revelation — it is already known
Must not contradict: box is in Jamie's bag, floorboard has been replaced and looks undisturbed, camera is also in the bag
Voss has not yet asked about the bag directly — she only glanced at it; this episode may escalate that tension
The box has not been opened yet — its contents are unknown until this episode
key_events: Voss asks, with exaggerated calm, whether Jamie has come across anything 'unusual' in the house — her eyes still carrying the weight of that glance toward the floorboard. Jamie says no, just old rubbish, nothing worth keeping. Voss holds the pause one beat too long before giving him a Friday deadline and telling him to call her immediately if he finds anything; her voice is pleasant but the instruction feels like a warning. As she moves toward the door, her eyes pass over the bag on the floor. She says nothing about it and leaves. [THEREFORE Jamie is alone — BUT Voss's glance at the bag tells him the question was not really about the floorboard: she suspects the box may be gone]

Jamie waits by the bay window until Voss's car turns off Crane Street, then kneels beside the bag and takes out the metal box. The lid is sealed tight — rust has fused the lock and filled the small gap around the seam. He takes a flat-head screwdriver from his tool bag and works it into the edge. The metal resists. He tries again with more force. [THEREFORE he is working to open it — BUT the difficulty stretches the tension and gives him time to think about what Voss just said]

The lock gives with a dull crack. Jamie opens the box. Inside, wrapped in a piece of folded cloth, are three black-and-white photographs and a small envelope. He examines the photographs one by one: they show the front of the Crane Street house, but the windows are dark with smoke marks and the plaster around the frames is scorched. In one photograph, two figures stand near the front step, but their faces are turned away from the camera. [THEREFORE the photographs point clearly to a fire at this house — BUT the envelope is what Jamie reaches for next]

Jamie opens the envelope and unfolds the single sheet inside. The handwriting is tight and slanted, the ink faded to brown. At the top, a date: 14 February 1974. Below it, a name: R. Voss. The note is short — no more than eight lines — but its contents are unmistakable: it describes an arrangement between two parties to 'clear the Crane Street property' using fire, and refers to the Warner family leaving before it happens. Jamie reads it twice. He turns it over. There is no other signature, no address. He sets it down on the floorboard beside him and stares at it. The name Voss is written in the same careful hand as the rest of the note — not added later, not accidental. It was always there. [THEREFORE this is real, physical evidence of planned arson connecting the Voss name to the Warners' disappearance — BUT Jamie now faces a harder question: Voss told him to call her immediately if he found anything, and he suspects she already knows the box is missing]
major_reveal: The handwritten note in the box, dated February 1974 and addressed to 'R. Voss', describes a deliberate plan to clear the Crane Street house by fire — linking the Voss family directly to the Warner family's disappearance fifty years ago.
threads_advanced: thread_004: Jamie now knows exactly what he is hiding from Voss, and the stakes are much higher — this is not just a box but evidence of arson

thread_002: Voss's glance at the bag in this episode, combined with her pointed warning, makes it more strongly implied — though still not confirmed — that she is the one who disturbed the floorboard and knows the box should have been there
threads_resolved: thread_005: the metal box has been opened; its contents are now known — three photographs showing fire damage at the Crane Street house and a handwritten note addressed to R. Voss describing a plan to commit arson in February 1974
new_thread_created: The note is addressed to 'R. Voss' — who is R. Voss, what is their relationship to Margaret, and how much does Margaret know about what the note says?
tone_guardrails: The revelation comes through a physical object — a handwritten note — not through dialogue or exposition; keep it concrete
Only one major revelation: the Voss name on the arson note; do not also confirm Voss disturbed the floorboard in this episode
Jamie's reaction should be quiet and unsettled, not dramatic — he is a young man sitting alone in an empty room with something he did not expect to find
Voss's scene must carry subtext, not explicit threat — her politeness is the danger
forbidden_moves: Do not confirm that Voss was the person who disturbed the floorboard — this remains strongly implied but unconfirmed until a later episode
Do not reveal who R. Voss is or their relationship to Margaret — that question is introduced here and answered later
Do not have Jamie contact the police or Mrs. Holt in this episode — the ending should leave him alone with the decision
Do not re-explain who the Warners were — the reader already knows this from Episode 2
ending_hook: Jamie is sitting alone on the bare floorboard, holding a note that names Voss in a plan to burn this house down fifty years ago. Voss has just told him to call her the moment he finds anything. He knows what she wants — and he knows she may already suspect he has it.
